abstract:
Transatlantic data transfers for health research have become increasingly fraught due to the friction between the EU’s stringent General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and US mass surveillance frameworks. A primary concern remains the broad access granted to US law enforcement and national security agencies over personal data originating in the EU. Since the GDPR took effect, a growing body of evidence shows that critical scientific collaborations are being stalled or abandoned, with public institutions in the US facing the most significant hurdles. 

The adoption of a new EU-US Data Privacy Framework in July 2023 offers little hope for a permanent fix, as it faces the same legal vulnerabilities that led the Court of Justice of the EU to invalidate previous agreements. With the research community urgently seeking stable alternatives, the proposed European Health Data Space (EHDS) regulation may offer a new pathway to reconciliation. This paper provides a critical analysis of the current legal landscape and proposes a viable strategy for facilitating data flow between public bodies through the specialized mechanisms of the EHDS.
